text: David Chalmers sharpened Nagel's insight into what he called the 'hard problem of consciousness': explaining not just why the brain processes information (the 'easy problems') but why any of that processing is accompanied by subjective experience at all. The easy problems — attention, memory integration, verbal reporting — are not trivial, but they yield, in principle, to functional explanation. The hard problem does not. Why should electrical activity in the visual cortex produce the vivid redness of a rose rather than occurring in the dark? That question, Chalmers argued, remains open even after every neural mechanism is mapped.
question: According to Chalmers, what makes the 'hard problem' of consciousness distinct from the 'easy problems'?

options.text: The hard problem involves more complex neural circuits than the easy problems
The easy problems are unsolvable, while the hard problem can be addressed with better instruments
The easy problems can be solved by explaining functions, but the hard problem asks why processing is accompanied by subjective experience at all
The hard problem concerns attention and memory, while easy problems concern visual perception
